data.title: National Vision Holdings: Growth Outlook Got Better
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about National Vision Holdings, Inc. (EYE) previously with a buy rating as the turnaround story progressed in the right direction, and I expected further upside given the growth catalysts in place for 2H25. My view today is even more bullish. My reason is simple. Managed care is working, premiumization is extending beyond frames, and remote hybrid exams could help ease a real capacity bottleneck.

The customer mix is getting better

It has been ~7 months since my last update, and I think the biggest change is that EYE is no longer a turnaround story that still needs proof. For those who do not follow, the FY2025 results indicate that EYE is actually growing very healthily (not what you would expect from a business that is still deep in a turnaround phase). Net revenue from continuing operations grew 9% to $1.99 billion; adjusted comparable store sales grew 6%, and adjusted EBIT margin improved by 160 bps y/y to 5.2%. This is not a case where the topline accelerated, but earnings dragged. Both moved in the right direction.

More importantly, it is where these improvements came from. Back then, my view was that managed care would grow into a major growth driver, and indeed, that came true. Managed care comp sales grew low double-digits in FY2025, and management even said they ended the year with 42% of revenue attributable to managed care. Yes, total traffic for the year did come down 0.5%, but that was mostly due to a fall in self-pay customers, which doesn't matter since EYE gained traffic in more profitable cohorts. Then, if you combine this with the fact that progressive and outside Rx customers, on top of managed care, were up high-single digits on comp, with traffic for those customers up mid-single digits, it is clear that EYE is winning in the parts of the business that matter most. In other words, the quality of growth is improving. I would say this is a much better setup than relying on heavy discounting to drive volume from lower-value traffic. The current strategy works much better for the long term since better-quality customers usually mean better tickets (they spend more), better repeat behavior, and better margins.

Premiumization is no longer just about frames

The second big change is that EYE's premiumization strategy is no longer just about frames. For what it's worth, frames are still a big focus here, and EYE continues to do well. They ended FY2025 with ~40% of frames priced >$99, up from 20% at the start of the year. This change in mix appears to be well received by customers, as management also said some newer branded products were turning above market benchmarks. That tells me the improvement in customer mix (the point above) is real.

Aside from frames, the next leg of premiumization growth will be lenses, specifically add-ons like anti-reflective coating, advanced materials, transitions, and progressives (which all saw positive demand in FY2025). The plan for 2026 is to do what EYE already did on frames. For instance, include more premium lenses in the assortment, push for better pricing, and also simplify the assortment; launch a new tier (i.e., the Tier 4 progressive lens). EYE is also going to roll out Stellest, a premium product. The goal is to push premium materials penetration to ~60% from 50% today and the anti-reflective mix to ~50% from ~40% today.

The core assumption here is that consumers would buy these premium lenses, which I think they will. The way I see it, premium lenses are usually easier to sell because there is a clear functional benefit, not just a nicer look (like frames). Things like anti-reflective coating, better materials, and progressive lenses all improve day-to-day use, and consumers can feel it in real time (which gives them a clearer reason to trade up). The impact on EYE P&L is that this should lift the average ticket, which should flow through to better margins.

Remote hybrid exam

One new catalyst that I figured out is the remote hybrid exam model. In the US, exam capacity is one of the real constraints in optical retail. The lack of exam capacity also means weaker customer conversion since the customer wouldn't know what lens degree to purchase. With exam capacity, EYE can improve marketing, upgrade assortment, and bring in better customers, but that doesn't work if the stores cannot support exam demand consistently. That is why I think this remote model matters. With the remote hybrid exam model, it basically eases the constraint since doctors can be “utilized” across multiple stores without being physically there. EYE is doubling down on this and already has remote telehealth capability installed in >800 locations. This should give EYE more flexibility in how it uses doctor capacity across the fleet and reduce the risk that demand gets lost simply because exam coverage is not there.

Valuation

We are back to the low end of EYE's valuation range (NTM P/E) over the past 3 years, which I think is a good entry point for investors who have missed the previous rally. If you look at the small chart in the left picture, you would see that historically, consensus estimates have been fairly accurate, if not underestimating actual performance. Current consensus estimates call for adjusted EPS to grow from $0.70 to $1.80 over 4 years, which is about ~27% CAGR. For a business that compounds adjusted EPS by high-20%, I think a 23x NTM P/E doesn't make sense since that is near where the current S&P 500 index trades at (19.5x NTM P/E with mid-teens adjusted EPS CAGR). EYE should trade at a decent premium given its growth profile, so if we assume EYE reverts back to the mean (28.5x), the stock is easily worth $51.

Risk

If the next leg of premiumization fails to work, i.e., attachment rates on higher-end lens products disappoint, the runway for higher ticket and margin expansion could look less optimistic than expected. The ramp-up in remote exam capacity would also add fixed costs to the business. If demand fails to come through as expected, margins could stay weak for the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for EYE. Things have developed in the right direction. Managed care is driving up customer quality, premiumization now has a second leg in lenses, and remote hybrid exams could help unlock the next stage of growth by easing exam capacity constraints. At the same time, the stock is back near the low end of its recent NTM P/E range, which I think gives investors a reasonable entry point.

data.title: Franklin Intermediate Municipal SMA Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: In the fourth quarter of 2025 (Q4), new issuance of municipal (muni) bonds increased 14% year-over-year compared to 2024, culminating in a new annual record with tax-exempt issuance surpassing $500 billion. By quarter end, the yield on the benchmark 10-year United States Treasury (UST) note moved two basis points (bps) higher to 4.17%, and the yield on the 30-year UST bond increased by 11 bps to 4.84%. The mid-November resolution of the US federal government shutdown supported sentiment through year-end. The Fed policy also drove overall returns, with three consecutive 25 bps rate cuts which were well received by the market. However, the future path of cuts grew ambiguous as Fed members were split on the need for further adjustments.
  • Contributors: Underweight duration exposure.
  • Detractors: Overweight allocation to AAA rated bonds.
  • Outlook: Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

Performance Review

  • Over the quarter, Franklin Intermediate Municipal SMA Composite underperformed its benchmark (gross and net of fees), Bloomberg Managed Money Intermediate Index.
  • The strategy's duration, which is shorter than that of the benchmark, aided relative returns for the period due to overweight to muni bonds with two or less years to maturity as muni yields in the longer-maturity portion of the muni yield curve increased.
  • An overweight to AAA rated muni bonds, along with an underweight to AA rated munis, detracted from performance during the period as lower-rated issues modestly performed better than their higher-rated peers. Drawing on the expertise of our dedicated municipal research team, we remain focused on issuers that we believe possess strong fiscal underpinnings.

Outlook

  • While Q4 muni bond issuance exceeded historical averages, the pace of issuance slowed going into year-end; nonetheless, full-year issuance exceeded last year's already elevated levels. Looking ahead to 2026, demand will play a crucial role as supply is projected to reach exceptional highs, primarily driven by infrastructure capital requirements that can no longer depend on previous government stimulus programs alone. This provides a stronger technical backdrop as demand has also increased over the past several months. The muni-bond yield curve remained relatively stable during the quarter.
  • Fundamentals remain stable; however, slower economic growth will limit expansion in revenues across many sectors, in particular tax revenues. Rainy day funds remain healthy at the state and local government levels, which bolster fiscal resilience despite ongoing expense growth.
  • Municipal bond valuations continue to appear attractive, particularly on a tax-adjusted basis. Yields remain above five-year averages, allowing investors to lock in compelling income. In a more challenging environment, strong bottom-up research and disciplined security selection will be essential to identifying relative value and preserving portfolio quality.

data.title: DoorDash: Buy The Leading Local Commerce Platform At A Discount
data.description:
Over the past few years, DoorDash (DASH) has fundamentally proven its ability to scale while maintaining a path toward massive profitability. The stock has sold down 45%+ from its all-time highs. I believe this is an opportunity to buy a hyper-growth platform at a bargain. The sell-off was caused by agentic disruption fears and new growth investments, then fueled by the general market correction and higher gas prices for drivers. These problems are either misunderstood or are short-term headwinds, while the fundamentals continue to improve. I rank DASH as a Strong Buy with a fair value of $175.

The main concerns that the market had from its Q4 earnings came from its bias towards sacrificing short-term margin expansion for accelerated growth. Investments require execution and cause margins to be compressed. However, I believe DASH is positioning itself to have durable growth and margin expansion driven by geographic expansion, operating leverage, and its advertising business.

Business Overview

DoorDash runs at an incredible scale, completing 903 million orders last quarter, up 32% YoY. The recent spike in performance came from the inclusion of its Deliveroo acquisition.

DoorDash is more than just a restaurant delivery app. It is the infrastructure for local e-commerce. The market is underestimating their aggressive expansion into grocery and retail (G&R), with 30%+ of MAU using it and expected profitability later this year. DASH now consists of three main global pillars: DoorDash for North America and Australia, Wolt scaling throughout Central and Eastern Europe, and the massive $3.9 billion acquisition of Deliveroo for Western Europe and the Middle East. Then DASH drives higher frequency and retention with a monthly subscription, with over 35 million people holding DashPass and Wolt+.

An underappreciated portion of DASH is their advertising business. Their apps are virtual real estate, and restaurants will pay for the best spot. Brands can also pay for pop-ups or placements; this is especially powerful for G&R. In 2024, DoorDash and Wolt ad revenue crossed $1b across 30+ countries and 150,000+ advertisers. With its high margins and fast growth, advertising will be a meaningful driver for bottom-line growth.

Moat

Despite a recent Substack article that sent DASH stock tumbling, I don't believe agents or "vibe-coding" pose an existential threat. While the technology required to operate at a billions-of-trips scale is impressive, the software itself was never DoorDash's actual moat. Many well-funded competitors have tried and failed, leaving US food delivery consolidated down to DoorDash, Uber Eats, and a small amount to GrubHub.

The reason DoorDash has a dominant ~68% market share in the US is the flywheel effect. Logistics is a game of scale. Whoever has the highest liquidity of couriers, merchants, and consumers creates the fastest and most reliable experience. In 2025 alone, DoorDash generated nearly $75 billion in sales for local merchants across 40+ countries, supported by a fleet of millions of active couriers. When you combine that massive supply-side infrastructure with over 56 million monthly active users driving relentless demand, the network effect locks in. Much like the dynamic that protects Uber in rideshare, it is a very capital-intensive effort to compete and take meaningful market share.

Growth Investments

DASH management believes a unified tech platform and cheaper forms of delivery will streamline and accelerate the business over time. Right now, DASH operates with the DoorDash, Wolt, or Deliveroo tech stack, depending on the market. They are working on three different platforms instead of one. Over this year, DASH is investing to streamline the backend, which should drive efficiencies and accelerate broad product development. Onto the physical delivery, autonomous drivers will eventually be a cheaper alternative to humans. The biggest friction to using DoorDash is the extra fees and tips, which go towards the drivers, the cost of convenience. Robots don't need tips and can even use advertising to reduce operating costs. If the cost of delivery drops, engagement will improve while DoorDash keeps the same cut. These investments and partnerships are already happening: Serve sidewalk robots are currently delivering orders in LA, multiple delivery drone pilots, and DoorDash's own custom delivery robot called Dot piloting in Phoenix.

Financials

DASH has transitioned from burning cash for many years to being profitable. The growth has not been slowing down either, despite its $102 billion Gross Order Value (GOV) scale. In Q4, revenue grew 26% without the Deliveroo acquisition. With the acquisition, revenue grew 38% organically in line with GOV. DASH has a long runway between category growth, international growth, and G&R expansion.

2025 was a breakout year for profitability, net income reaching a positive $935 million. The top-line growth is dropping straight to the bottom line alongside margin expansion from operating leverage and the ads business. Last quarter, net income jumped 51% to $213 million. The current 6.7% net income margins are immature. For example, Uber Eats has around an 18% non-GAAP operating margin (including SBC, without one-time impacts).

Valuation

DoorDash has shown food delivery is a stable and fast-growing business, now extending into G&R and more countries across the globe. DASH should continue its growth path and drive meaningful bottom-line expansion.

If revenue grows 18% over the next 5 years with a net income margin of 20% in 2030, DoorDash will be producing $6.3b of profits. The current market cap is only around $64b. That would mean EPS will compound a whopping 47% yearly, assuming a flat share count. With a 25x P/E for its growth, DASH will be worth $350 in 2030. My hurdle rate for stocks is 15%, better than the usual 10% from the S&P 500. That means DoorDash's fair value is currently $175 with 20% immediate upside.

These base-case assumptions are conservative. 18% revenue CAGR factors in a natural deceleration from their current 38% growth rate as the law of large numbers takes effect. Assuming a 20% net income margin by 2030 simply brings DoorDash in line with the mature segments of Uber Eats (which currently operate near an 18-20% non-GAAP margin), with further upside driven by their advertising segment. Lastly, assigning a 25x P/E multiple is a fair, if not cheap, premium for a high-quality platform company.

Metric

Base Case Assumption

EPS (TTM)

 $2.12

Proj. EPS/share Growth Rate (CAGR)

47%

Terminal GAAP multiple

25x

2030 Projected EPS

~$14.3

2030 Stock Price Target

~$350

Implied Return (CAGR)

~19%

Risks

DoorDash is a consumer discretionary company; any economic downturn could lead to slow growth. However, food is on the lower end of the list people cut, and DASH is a diversified company through demography and geographies. Delivery is also an intensely competitive industry. Uber is dominating globally with its leading market share and cross-platform advantage. For example, DASH has recently shut down 4 markets where they no longer believe they can win in the long term. The costs and integration of DoorDash buying its growth in new markets have their execution risks as well.

Conclusion

DoorDash should continue to see strong growth as it expands local e-commerce across its brands and the globe, while its moat is strengthened by the flywheel effect and scale. I rank DoorDash as a strong buy with a 1-year price target of at least $175.

In the short run, the market is a voting machine, but in the long run, it is a weighing machine" - Benjamin Graham

data.title: Paysign Is On A Roll
data.description:
We've been flashing Paysign, Inc. (PAYS) as a buy (adding at $3.14 recently for our members), as we didn't really understand why the stock was falling, given that it has two businesses that are doing well.

  • Its cash cow, Plasma Centers, has a 50% market share and is only growing as it adds plenty of new centers (115 in FY25 to 595 centers at the end of the year). Revenue per center is actually down, as there is a surplus of plasma, and collection efficiency increased by 10%.
  • The hypergrowth is in its Patient Affordability segment, which is very fortunate because that has a much bigger TAM ($500M-$850M with $1B possible through adding new features) and produces higher margins to boot.

We assumed that the share price fall was related to the headwinds in the Plasma Center business, which is still bigger than Patient Affordability (although they will even out sometime this year).

However, it turns out that even the Plasma Center segment is growing at a very decent 16.7% in Q4 (+4% for FY25 as a whole). That might change as the growth comes from adding new plasma centers, and management guided that it will only add a few new centers in FY26 (in fact, Q1 will see a decline of 4 centers to 591 centers).

On the other hand, if inventory levels come down, we could see a rise in revenue per center.

Patient Affordability

  • This is the company's growth business, and grow it does.
  • Management argues it's still in the first inning.

The business manages co-pays for people on private insurance. There are 160M people with private insurance, so this is a large opportunity. The company entered this segment 3 years ago when it was a commodity business and added something new (Q4CC):

There's a market for co-pay. We have a better mousetrap than anybody else has, and it's showing up in the numbers.

Indeed, the numbers show exactly that, with FY25 revenue up 167.8% to $33.9M, adding 55 programs and (Q4CC):

a corresponding increase in monthly management fees, setup fees, claim processing fees, and other billable services such as dynamic business rules and customer service contact center support.

The competitive advantage from the Q4CC:

Our patient affordability platform helps pharmaceutical companies ensure patients can access high-cost medications by administering co-pay assistance programs. In 2025, our platform helped deliver nearly $1 billion in financial assistance to patients, supporting access to high-cost therapies for more than 840,000 individuals. At the same time, we help manufacturers better control how those dollars are spent, which is one of the key value propositions we provide. A key differentiator of our platform is our dynamic business rules technology, which helps pharmaceutical manufacturers avoid unnecessary costs associated with co-pay maximizer programs. In 2025 alone, this solution saved our clients over $325M. And this year, we have already saved our clients almost $150M.

They are a payments company because Paysign owns its processing switch and payment infrastructure; they provide pharma clients with a live look at the books, or “open book” pricing, which gave them a foothold in the market, which was “almost commoditized,” introducing something new.

Unlike the competition, it's an insignificant part of competitors' businesses, like Cencora and McKesson. The quote mentions DBR (Dynamic Business Rules).

These are proprietary algorithms that weed out so-called Maximizers and Accumulators, tactics used by insurance companies that drain co-payment resources from Paysign's pharma clients, money that was meant for patients, not for insurers.

And DBR works; it saved Paysign’s pharma clients $325M in 2025 alone and almost $150M in FY26 so far.

Management doesn't see risk from DTC platforms (like Hims & Hers), as these deal with products with little or no commercial insurance coverage.

Nor do they see danger from pharmacy discount programs (like GoodRx or TrumpRx) as these (Q4CC):

serve an important role in reducing costs for lower-priced generic medications or for those patients without insurance. They are not designed for nor do they compete with branded specialty medications, where commercial insurance and co-pay programs are the standard of care.

AI isn't considered a threat, and management is implementing LLMs to strengthen DBR algorithms.

Management expects to pick up a GLP-1 program in the next 12 to 18 months through existing client relationships. Although these are high-volume DTC businesses, there is not too much insurance or co-pay involved, at least not yet. Investors need to understand that Paysign earns most from specialty meds (Q4CC):

Sure, there's a lot of volume there, but your chance to make -- to kind of add on the additional functionality that can generate larger revenue is just not there on the retail side, which is one of the reasons we highly target the specialty space because we can make far more money on 1,000 DBR claims than we can on, say, 20,000 retail claims.

And they should also consider this (Q4CC, our emphasis):

I think we're in the first inning. There's still a lot of growth potential here. We don't see anything slowing down when it comes to new program acquisition. And if you look at the growth that we're doing year-over-year and not just from a dollar perspective, right, just from also throwing in the number of programs that we're adding in. Last year, it was 1 every 6-point-something days we were putting a new program up. Hopefully, this year, we will have similar metrics as far as the number of programs that we're pulling in. But it's -- we're nowhere near the middle of this at all. We're very much in the beginning. And I think we'll continue to see very strong growth out of this vertical for many years to come.

The selling cycle for new programs is 90 days, on average, a little faster with existing clients adding new programs and a little slower with new pharma companies.

Plasma

The plasma center business grew 4% in FY25 to $45.6M (and 16.7% to $12.6M in Q4) through adding 115 new centers (totaling 595 centers) as average revenue per center declined as a result of high plasma inventory levels and hardware upgrades increasing collection efficiency by some 10%.

While enjoying a near 50% market share already, there is room for expansion (Q4CC):

We continue to engage the remaining plasma collection companies that are currently not our customers. We believe our expanded suite of donor management and engagement tools, which we acquired last year, creates additional opportunities to grow our footprint in this space.

BECS

The BECS (Blood Establishment Computer System) is Paysign's new donor management system, which is currently under FDA 510(k) review, with management expecting feedback from the FDA within the next 60 days.

Management is integrating the BECS with various plasmapheresis devices to ensure seamless installations once cleared.

This could be a very significant development as it evolves the company from taking a small fee on payouts to donors to becoming a SaaS company charging subscription fees as the BECS manages stuff like donor eligibility, health screenings, deferral lists, and regulatory compliance.

Once a plasma center integrates Paysign’s BECS, they are unlikely to leave, as having the BECS and the payment processor in one single platform is a major efficiency gain for centers. This creates a massive moat around its existing plasma business, which holds nearly 50% market share in the US.

Apart from increasing the stickiness of customers, SaaS subscriptions will also likely increase gross margins.

Payroll

One would almost forget, but the company has a third segment, payroll, retail, and corporate incentive programs, which increased by $671K (+36.2%) to $2.52M.

The core of their payroll offering is the partnership with Spentra, a fintech focused on EWA (Earned Wage Access), enabling employees to access their earned wages immediately via a Paysign-issued Mastercard rather than wait weeks for their paychecks.

This EWA instrument is marketed as a retention tool for employers in high-turnover industries like hospitality and retail, integrating directly with existing HR and payroll software, making it a low-friction add-on for Paysign's corporate clients.

The cards include early direct deposit (up to 2 days), purchase protection, and a mobile app for real-time balance tracking.

This segment simply produces economies of scope for Paysign as it uses existing engines.

Finances

  • FY25 revenue was up 40.5% to $82M.
  • Net income increased 98% to $7.6M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA rose 107% to $19.9M.
  • Operating margins expanded by 723 basis points to 9% in FY25.
  • Gross margin improved to 59.4% compared to 55.1% in FY24.
  • Paysign exited the year with $21.1M in cash, nearly double the previous year, with zero bank debt.

Outlook

  • FY26 revenue is projected to be between $106.5M and $110.5M (30% to 35% growth), with plasma and pharma expected to contribute roughly equal dollar amounts.
  • Net income will nearly double to $13M to $16M ($0.21 to $0.26 per diluted share).
  • Adjusted EBITDA is expected in the range of $30M to $33M ($0.49 to $0.53 per diluted share).
  • OpEx is expected to increase only 20%, significantly trailing revenue growth, as the company reaches a plateau in fixed costs.
  • Q1/26 revenue is estimated at $27M to $27.5M, with an expected exit count of 137 pharma programs and 589 plasma centers (losing 6 through an acquisition of a client).

Valuation

The fully diluted share count for FY26 was estimated by management to be 62.3M, which yields a market cap (at $5.5 per share) of $342.65M and an EV of $321.5M, resulting in an FY26 EV/S of just under 3x. Analysts expect FY26 EPS to be $0.24, rising to $0.31 for FY27.

Risk

We have to admit we're hard-pressed to come up with a significant risk, although it's always possible that some company comes up with a better mousetrap.

There don't seem to be many new center additions in the plasma business, but that's already in the guidance.

Investors might worry about the ballooning AR account, jumping from $32.6M at the end of 2024 to $72.2M by the end of 2025, as the company usually pays pharmacies before they are reimbursed by big pharma; rapid growth drains their unrestricted cash. In 2025, they saw a significant impact on cash flow due to this timing, but they seem to have some margin here:

In fact, management expects $3.1M in interest income for FY26, which doesn't sound like impending cash flow problems to us.

Conclusion

  • The hypergrowth in Patient Affordability is set to continue for some time to come; management argues that they are still in the first inning.
  • Patient Affordability becoming bigger (reaching 50% of revenue in FY26) is producing gross margin expansion, with operating leverage even accelerating, so bottom line metrics should grow substantially faster than the top-line growth.
  • While the Plasma business isn't a fast grower (5% under normal circumstances, according to management), and there is still an inventory glut, the company has added a whopping 115 new centers, so when that glut disappears, we can expect even this segment to produce significant growth.
  • The Plasma business is likely to receive a considerable boost from the BECS, creating high-margin SaaS subscription revenues, increasing the revenue per center.
  • Even their third segment, Payroll, had a very good year with 36% growth, albeit from a small basis.
  • We were baffled at the continuous stock price declines before Q4 and added shares at $3.14.

data.title: French Inflation Picks Up As The Economy Stalls
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

Inflation rebounds

As anticipated, consumer prices rebounded in France in March, rising by 1.7% year-on-year after 0.9% in February. The harmonised index, closely watched by the ECB, stood at 1.9%, compared with 1.1% the previous month. This renewed increase in inflation is unsurprisingly linked to the situation in the Middle East. In the wake of higher oil prices, energy inflation jumped to 7.3% YoY, after a decline of 2.9% in February. The increase in gas prices is not yet reflected in consumer prices due to the structure of gas contracts.

Excluding energy, French inflation remains contained. Food price inflation slowed to 1.8% YoY from 2% the previous month. Prices of manufactured goods continue to decline, falling by 0.6% after a 0.2% decline in February. Services inflation edged up slightly to 1.7%, from 1.6% the previous month.

Overall, the conflict in the Middle East represents a major shock to the global economy and, by extension, to the French economy. The exact magnitude of the shock remains uncertain and will depend on the duration and intensity of the conflict. But one thing is clear: each passing day increases the risks to both growth and inflation. The figures published today confirm that a first inflationary wave is already underway. After fuel prices, second-round effects via transport costs and certain industrial goods should not be long in coming. The key question now is whether this will remain a temporary shock or mark the beginning of broader-based inflation, with de-anchoring of inflation expectations and stronger wage demands.

The risk of an inflationary wave is limited in France

The risk of an inflationary spiral nevertheless remains more limited in France than in the rest of the euro area. The starting point for inflation is lower than in most other European countries. The French economy is characterised by a large share of administered prices or prices negotiated in a centralised manner, particularly in large-scale retailing, which slows the pass-through of cost increases to consumer prices. In addition, economic conditions prior to the conflict were already weak, with constrained demand.

The labour market remains fragile: hiring intentions are low and net job creation has been negative for several quarters. Wage negotiations for this year do not incorporate this new inflationary shock. In this context, wage growth should remain more moderate than elsewhere, limiting price pressures, particularly in services. Even in the case of a “one-off” inflationary shock, however, French inflation should average around 2% in 2026, compared with a forecast of 1.3% for the harmonised index prior to the conflict.

Economic activity engines are already under strain

Other data released today confirms that the risks weighing on French activity are significant. The year 2026 started on a slightly more optimistic note, but on fragile foundations. The first activity data for the beginning of the year is disappointing. Services production fell by 0.6% month-on-month in January. Retail sales volumes stagnated. Household consumption contracted sharply in February, down 1.4% on the month after an increase of 0.4% in January.

These indicators point to a weaker-than-expected first quarter and an already fragile starting point, even before the economic effects of the conflict fully materialise. Growth could therefore surprise on the downside as early as the first quarter, while the conflict will weigh on consumption, investment and production in the quarters ahead. In this context, GDP growth well below expectations at the start of the year now appears likely. Our March forecast of average growth of 0.7% for the full year (compared with 1% expected at the beginning of the year) now already looks optimistic.

At a time when the government was targeting a budget deficit close to 5% this year, based on GDP growth of 1%, this new shock risks once again weighing on tax revenues and further deteriorating public finances. Limited fiscal space significantly reduces the likelihood of large-scale support measures to cushion the rise in energy prices, unlike what happened in 2022.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Lionsgate: Slate Diversification Isn't Leading To Revenue Growth
data.description:
In a very choppy stock market environment in 2026, investors have to be careful to deploy active portfolio management to weed out weak companies from our portfolios. Persistent inflation, a weak consumer spending environment, and the possibility of a less-accommodating Fed are all threats on the horizon that will challenge businesses that have already entered into the year facing pressure.

Lionsgate Studios Corp. (LION), the Hollywood studio known best for the Hunger Games and John Wick series, has been struggling over the past year to keep generating box office hits and return to top-line growth while managing an overbearing debt load. Despite its challenges, shares have risen nearly ~40% over the past year, ever since the company spun off its streaming arm, Starz Entertainment Corp. (STRZ), into a separate publicly traded company. Can Lionsgate truly justify its gains over the past year, or is this a phantom rally that is unlikely to last in a choppy market?

I last wrote a “Sell” rating on Lionsgate in January, when the stock was trading at similar levels around $9. Since then, the company has reported continued profit contraction, while adjusted OIBDA has also shrunk and led to rising leverage ratios. It appears that Lionsgate is leaning heavily on its back library to drive revenue, while recent releases aren't picking up the slack as we wait for major tentpole releases ahead in FY27. I'm reiterating my “Sell” rating here.

Weaker film profitability, lower TV revenue

Lionsgate last reported fiscal Q3 (December quarter) results in early February, and though the stock has rallied modestly since that print, I found many issues that make the stock risky to hold through the rest of the year. The company's trended results are shown in the chart below:

Let's start with the issues in the company's motion picture (box office films) division. In Q3, motion picture revenue did grow 35% y/y to $421 million. The growth was driven by the latest installment of Now You See Me: Now You Don't, the third film in the franchise (whose first film kicked off in 2013), with a star-studded ensemble cast including Jesse Eisenberg, Woody Harrelson, and Morgan Freeman. The film grossed nearly $250 million at the box office and had a favorable comparison against a relatively emptier slate in the year-ago quarter.

Despite the sharp revenue growth, segment profit in the film division fell 29% y/y to just $59 million, or a 14% segment margin. The company blamed the margin decay on the timing of P&A spending (print and advertising, essentially movie promotion costs) to support titles that released in the back half of Q3 (and continued grossing well into Q4): Now You See Me, Good Fortune (which grossed only $26 million against a $30 million budget, a clear flop), and The Housemaid starring Sydney Sweeney (which has grossed $397 million and did markedly better). We're hoping for a catch-up in profitability in Q4, but overall, we're disappointed that OIBDA is declining. We note that P&A spending ahead of tentpoles is the nature of the business, and management has also warned that Q4 will include spending to support the April (Q1) release of Michael (a biographical drama on Michael Jackson's rise to fame).

Meanwhile, on the TV studio side, revenue fell 25% y/y, while segment profit also fell 8% y/y. The company notes that many of its new releases, including new pilots like The Rainmaker, amassed healthy viewership and that the company has secured coveted renewals across all of its new titles. Per CEO Jon Feltheimer's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

In television, our series continue to perform well across every platform. The Studio, which just began shooting its second season for Apple TV, was one of the most critically acclaimed shows of the year. The Hunting Wives was Netflix's top non-original English language series for the second half of last year and debuted high on their global list of top 10 shows despite only airing on Netflix in the U.S.

The Rainmaker was USA Network's most-watched freshman series in 7 years. Robinhood has ranked #1 on MGM+ for 9 weeks in a row, and The Rookie has been resurgent in its eighth season on ABC. The Rookie: North spin-off pilot begins shooting in Vancouver later this month, and Spartacus: House of Ashur is one of the best-reviewed series on Starz with a 92% Rotten Tomatoes rating and performing well across its international platforms.

And in a business where renewals are the name of the game, the renewal of nearly every one of our scripted shows anchors a fiscal '27 slate with double the number of scripted episode deliveries and a diversified mix of cost-plus and retained rights models, balancing profitability with long-term value creation.

Yet we have to ask the pertinent question: When will we see Lionsgate return to meaningful revenue growth here? The good news is that Lionsgate continues to monetize its huge back library, with the company noting that TTM revenue from library titles grew 10% y/y to $1.05 billion (37% of total company revenue of $2.83 billion over the past year). That said, we're growing increasingly uncomfortable with the company's reliance on its library to drive revenue. We're seeing the company talk up its latest hits, but we're not seeing these hits translate into revenue growth or profit expansion.

Debt is pushing higher toward uncomfortable levels

Meanwhile, there's also the burgeoning balance sheet to consider. As of the end of Q3, Lionsgate's net debt rose to $1.75 billion, a slight increase sequentially versus Q2. Meanwhile, net leverage ratios expanded to a multi-year high at 7.4x, a one-turn increase from 6.4x at the end of Q2 and also slightly higher than 6.9x in the prior-year Q3.

The increase in leverage ratios is due primarily to a decline in the OIBDA denominator, as the company faced profit headwinds in both its motion picture and television segments. As a reminder here, commercial banks typically consider a ~3x leverage ratio to be the upper threshold of a highly leveraged company; lending to a company above that threshold is usually considered a speculative credit.

To me, that's precisely what Lionsgate is as an equity investment as well: heavy spending on moonshots, with a few hits that offset expensive flops. Beyond this, there's also risk from AI to consider. As Hollywood studios race to adopt AI for everything from editing and effects to writing, it remains to be seen how Lionsgate will respond and if it can remain competitive in a rapidly shifting industry.

Key takeaways

With Lionsgate shares rising ~40% over the past year, I see a perfect opportunity to lock in gains here. In my view, the company is overly reliant on the success of its current slate while having little room for error with such a leveraged balance sheet. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Microsoft: Back To Pre-COVID Multiples - But The Business Has Moved On
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) has returned to valuation levels last seen before the COVID period despite a business that, in my view, is stronger today with a more diversified revenue base and a leading position in AI infrastructure.

The recent multiple compression mainly reflects concerns around high AI-related capex and the sustainability of growth. I think the market is currently too focused on near-term margin pressure and not enough on how strong demand for compute, data, and enterprise software actually is.

At current levels, I view the risk/reward as attractive for long-term investors, although upside is likely to be more gradual than in prior cycles given the ongoing investments.

Company Overview

Microsoft is one of the most profitable large-cap software companies. This is mainly thanks to its scalable, recurring revenue model across its three core segments: Productivity and Business Processes, Intelligent Cloud, and More Personal Computing.

What is more interesting, however, is how different the profitability is. As shown in the chart above, margins in Productivity and Business Processes have expanded significantly over the past years, while More Personal Computing remains the lowest-margin business.

The reason is fairly straightforward. Margin expansion in Productivity and Business Processes, which includes Office 365 and AI-powered offerings, has been driven by a favourable business mix shift and monetization of AI features. Both helped operating leverage.

The more personal computing segment looks very different; it has a higher exposure to hardware, which is more capital-intensive and less scalable than software-based models. Also, weaker revenue trends in recent years have limited operating leverage.

The Intelligent Cloud segment, primarily driven by Azure, also generates high margins. However, ongoing investments in data centres and AI infrastructure have partly offset margin expansion. This dynamic is likely to continue in the coming years.

What Has Changed - And What Has Not

During its quarterly results this year, Microsoft spooked the market with a sharp increase in capex. Data centre spending rose by around 66%, while cloud growth came in slightly below expectations. This was despite strong demand for AI services.

Capex had already stepped up to approx. $80 billion in FY2025. Current expectations point to a level higher than $100 billion in FY2026 (approx. 30% of revenue). The exact number matters less than the direction: spending is clearly accelerating.

This has led to a clear shift in market perception:

  • Capex is increasingly seen as a risk, not simply an investment
  • Focus has shifted toward returns on capital
  • The narrative is moving from AI scarcity to potential overcapacity

In my view, the key concern is not that AI will fail, but that Microsoft is acting too fast, spending too much, which could lead to a "bubble" where AI adoption happens slower than their massive investments. This slower adoption could have a significant negative impact on the ROI of this AI capex and ultimately hurt margins through higher depreciation.

At the same time, fundamentals remain intact. Azure remains a growth driver (approx. 30% YoY), enterprise lock-in across Office, Dynamics, and the broader ecosystem remains intact (~17-19% YoY), and AI demand remains strong, with management highlighting ongoing capacity constraints. So, I don't see any sign that growth is in danger. The key risk is therefore not demand but timing, and the market may currently be over-penalising that uncertainty.

In my view, the current market interpretation is partly misleading. Capex of this magnitude is, in my view, more likely a response to visible demand growth and capacity constraints rather than speculative spending.

Valuation

As shown in the chart above, Microsoft's EV/EBITDA has moved back to levels seen before the COVID period despite a business that, in my view, is stronger today.

Let's briefly look at peers such as Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), Amazon.com (AMZN), and Oracle (ORCL). All are increasing capex to support AI-driven demand, and valuation differences across the group are relatively limited. To me, this mainly shows that the market is applying a similar framework across the sector with a strong focus on capex intensity and near-term returns.

On a relative basis, Microsoft does not seem as obviously cheap. The stock trades at around ~13x EBITDA. I find this notable, as Microsoft continues to deliver higher growth and profitability than most of its peers while operating at a significantly larger scale.

However, I think part of this is explained by the current investment cycle. Capex is expected to reach approx. $100 billion in 2026, which will likely lead to higher depreciation and potentially some pressure on margins in the near term. This helps explain why multiples have come down.

So, I see the key question not as whether Microsoft trades at a premium or discount to peers but if the market is correctly pricing the duration and impact of the current investment cycle.

There are, in my view, reasons why this pressure is more likely to be temporary.

First, current capex appears to be driven by capacity constraints and not speculative demand, as highlighted by management commentaries in the earnings call Q2/Q1 FY26, where demand for AI workloads continues to exceed available supply. Also more broadly, over 65% of S&P 500 companies referenced AI in their recent earnings calls. This implies that AI is not just a short-term theme but part of corporate strategies.

Second, a large part of the investment cycle is front-loaded. Revenue realisation is likely lagging AI functionality because you first need the infrastructure to more deeply integrate these functions into existing products and workflows.

Importantly, I think Microsoft is better positioned than most peers to absorb this cycle, given its ability to monetise across infrastructure, distribution, and applications.

As a result, the current valuation appears to assume a lengthier pressure on returns than what I think is likely to materialise. If AI demand continues to absorb capacity at the current pace, I think margin pressure should be temporary with the potential re-expansion of multiples over time.

Risks

There are some factors that could significantly change my investment thesis.

First, AI monetisation could take longer than expected. While I believe in the long-term opportunity, adoption could be slower than currently anticipated, delaying returns on Microsoft's elevated capex.

Second, capex remains high. If investment levels stay high for longer, margins and FCF could remain under pressure and justify part of the current market scepticism.

Third, competitive intensity could increase. Large peers are investing heavily into AI infrastructure as well, and more aggressive pricing or faster capacity build-outs could limit Microsoft's ability to translate demand into returns.

Conclusion

Microsoft remains a high-quality business with solid growth, strong margins, and a leading position in the ongoing AI build-out.

To me, the recent valuation compression mainly comes down to concerns around capex and the timing of returns, rather than any real change in demand. These concerns are fair, but I think they are more likely to be temporary.

At current levels, I view the market as over-penalising this uncertainty, which creates an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

I therefore rate Microsoft a Buy.

data.title: Follow The Photons -- The Case For CIEN As AI Infrastructure's Hidden Backbone
data.description:
Ciena (CIEN) has had an eye-watering rally in the past year with a return of 552% (~72% YTD) as it underwent a fundamental business transformation—from a telco vendor to a primary optical networking backbone for AI infrastructure. Having digested a prolonged inventory cycle in FY2024, the company has since entered a stage of steep revenue acceleration, posting a 33% Y/Y revenue growth in 1QFY26 while exiting the quarter with a $7B order backlog. As such, the company increased its FY2026 guidance, raising its revenue target to $5.9B-$6.3B, which implies a 28% Y/Y growth at the midpoint.

On March 30, Samsung Electronics is formally entering the silicon photonics foundry business, driving mid to high single digit percentage losses in Ciena -9.1% (CIEN), Lumentum -6.8% (LITE), and Coherent -9.8% (COHR). Through this vertical integration within Samsung Electronics, it could threaten commoditization of photonics and the long-term margins of CIEN, LITE and COHR. However, the ramp could take years, possibly by 2030, while the supply crunch is a present problem.

We eye the current pullback, which has been caused by general market weakness—the ongoing Middle Eastern conflict and oil crisis—and the knee-jerk reaction to the Samsung report, as an opportunity to buy CIEN.

Led by the AI optical infrastructure supercycle

AI workloads have set up an unimaginable level of demand pull for high bandwidth optical networking. GPU clusters used in AI training are interconnected with an optical fiber network in order to fulfill the need for the massive bandwidth.

The company expects its revenue to hit $5.9B to $6.3B in FY2026 with a 28% Y/Y growth rate, and qualitatively states that "long-term growth in core business plus new higher-growth addressable markets provide an opportunity to outpace [its] traditional revenue CAGR over the coming years" in its latest investor deck. We compute that its FY22-F25 revenue CAGR to be 9.5%, while sell-side consensus has FY2028E revenue at $8.77B, which implies a 23% CAGR. The global fiber optics market is forecast to reach $15.45B in 2032, reflecting a CAGR of 9.70% from 2025, according to Data Bridge Market Research.

The key insight that we draw from these numbers is that optical networking is meaningfully under-invested as hyperscalers have prioritized GPU capex and have neglected the fiber/optical layer. The inflection point is that there is insufficient bandwidth which constrains their ability to scale AI operations.

In our view, the management's revenue guidance is quite credible. In Fabrinet's (FN) 2QFY26 earnings call, Ciena's orders was specifically mentioned in helping drive its telecom revenue growth. Fabrinet is a primary contract manufacturer of Ciena, and the validation here speaks well for the manufacturing ramp. Also, CFO Mark Graff stated on the most recent call that nearly all orders taken now would be for FY27E, meaning that FY26E capacity is effectively sold out. The backlog of $7B exiting 1QFY26 grew $2B Q/Q, and is a strong evidence of robust revenue growth in the coming year.

Further, managed optical fiber network (MOFN) is an underappreciated opportunity where hyperscalers partner with CIEN to build and operate dedicated fiber transport infrastructure. The company has managed to secure landmark MOFN contracts in FY2025, which facilitates a stream of long-term, sticky revenue.

Competitive positioning and moat

Ciena's primary moat is WaveLogic (WL6) which is the industry's first 1.6 terabit/second coherent optical engine. Built on 3nm silicon, it operates on a single carrier wavelength. The competitive strength of WL6 draws from a 50% reduction in space and power per bit vs. last-gen 800G; 800G connectivity over distances up to 1,000 km; and 200GBaud modulation which is a first the industry.

The management believes that the WL6 platform represents an 18-24 month technological lead over the closest competition and has more than 90 global customers committed.

The most significant competitive development is Nokia's (NOK) $2.3B acquisition of Infinera which closed in 2025. The combined entity, through scaling of R&D expenses, can begin closing the gap on CIEN in the 1.2Tbps-1.6Tbps segment. However, Ciena has a sizable lead and will be moving on to WL7.

As mentioned in the opening, Samsung Electronics has laid out a roadmap to begin producing next-gen pluggable transceivers and co-packaged optics (CPO) by 2030. When vertically integrated, hyperscalers would have a lower barrier to design and produce their own optical chips, similar to Google's (GOOGL) TPUs and Amazon's (AMZN) Trainium. However, we believe the supply crunch in optical chips is a current bottleneck, and the timeframe in which Samsung enters is too late to affect near-term businesses for existing vendors in the next 2-3 years. Thus, we don't think the threat is credible enough apart from a knee-jerk reaction to sell off the stock.

There are other competitors worth mentioning but unlikely to pose any immediate threat. Huawei is dominant in China but with minimal ex-China presence, while Cisco (CSCO) is mainly focused on routing/switching and has limited coherent optics depth. Lumentum (LITE) is primarily a component supplier and not a competitor for systems which CIEN is in.

Financial analysis

During the past five years, the company has delivered a revenue CAGR of 7.1%, while adjusted EBITDA margins have been structurally compressed due to deliberate spending on WL6 and WL7 R&D development.

Period Revenue Gross Margin Adj. EBITDA Adj. EBITDA Margin Adj. EPS
FY2021 $3.62B 47.6% $703M 19.4% $2.91
FY2022 $3.63B 43.0% $502M 13.8% $1.90
FY2023 $4.39B 42.8% $666M 15.2% $2.72
FY2024 $4.01B 42.8% $481M 12.0% $1.82
FY2025 $4.77B 42.0% $637M 13.3% $2.64

Source: Company, Himalayas Research estimates

The company has a conservative net debt/adjusted EBITDA ratio of 0.68x. It has long-term debt of $1.52B vs. cash of $1.09B. A comfortable leverage ratio gives balance sheet optionality for M&A or continued share buybacks.

Risks

Customer concentration risk: the company is dominated by a small number of hyperscaler customers, each of which contributes to ~5% or more of total revenue. Any scale back in hyperscaler capex could directly hit CIEN.

Nokia/Infinera catching up: despite having 18-24 months of technology lead, CIEN's competitors could eventually bridge the gap, and threaten pricing of WL6/WL7.

New entrants such as Samsung could hit long term market share and profits, but it is unlikely to impact financials as the target timeframe is 2030.

Supply chain bottlenecks: hiccups in the supply chain could delay fulfillment and defers revenue recognition.

Valuation and verdict

We expect the company to deliver ~20% revenue CAGR from FY2025 to FY2028, with EBITDA margins significantly expanding from ~13% to ~25% over the same period. The company's margins have historically benefited from revenue expansion phases which is occurring now. Guidance for FY26E revenue, record backlog exiting FY2025, and total market growth estimates align well with a 20%+ growth rate.

Period Revenue EBITDA Adj. EPS FCF
FY2026E $6.12B $1.30B $7.48 $540M
FY2027E $7.35B $1.68B $9.31 $817M
FY2028E $8.77B $2.16B $11.20 $1.19B

Source: Himalayas Research estimates, market estimates

At about 61x forward earnings and 41x forward EV/EBITDA, CIEN leans strongly on the expensive side but does have a very robust revenue growth rate and backlog to support it. Moreover, the optic/fiber layer is heavily under-invested within AI infrastructure, while CIEN provides a strong product via WL6 that has a significant moat. This moat should be intact long enough to not be threatened by Samsung's entry in 2030.

We believe CIEN is a Buy and view the current pullback on geopolitical tensions and the knee-jerk selloff reaction to the Samsung report as a good opportunity to invest in the only U.S. coherent optics pureplay.

data.title: Columbia Small Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Small-cap U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.19% during the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 2000 Index. For comparison, large-cap U.S. equities returned 2.41% during the same period, as gauged by the Russell 1000 Index.

The positive three-month returns were primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year: better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble."

Within the small-cap universe, growth and value stocks returned 1.22% and 3.26% for the quarter as gauged by the Russell 2000 Growth Index and Russell 2000 Value Index, respectively.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

Carpenter Technology 2.96
RBC Bearings 2.92
Celsius Holdings 2.77
ICU Medical 2.44
Glaukos 2.23
AAON 2.03
InterDigital/PA 1.99
Rush Street Interactive 1.91
Hut 8 1.90
Bloom Energy 1.88

Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2025

Quarterly portfolio recap

Contributors to relative performance:

  • Lumentum Holdings (LITE) develops optical and photonics products, including high-speed optical modules and components used in data center telecommunication applications. Sentiment around Lumentum has benefited as Microsoft and other hyperscalers have moved forward with plans for continued massive spending, as they seek to upgrade their data centers. Lumentum posted earnings beat and raised guidance during the quarter, propelling the stock sharply higher. We view Lumentum as well positioned to continue to gain market share as AI data centers pursue higher processing speeds.
  • Coherent (COHR) is another AI connectivity-solutions provider that is benefiting from the shift to optical and photonics products. As with Lumentum, Coherent reported earnings that were above expectations and raised guidance, sending the stock notably higher. We believe that demand for the company's transceivers, used to enable rapid data transmission between component devices, will continue to accelerate alongside hyperscaler spending on AI data centers.
  • Hut 8 (HUT) is a vertically integrated digital infrastructure company that is in the process of executing a pivot away from a sole focus on bitcoin mining and toward supporting the high-performance computing needs of hyperscalers — companies such as Microsoft, Alphabet and Amazon that are building out data centers at a massive scale to support cloud services and AI applications. As with Bitcoin mining, the operation of these data centers is extremely energy intensive. Hut 8 is well positioned to help power-hungry hyperscalers via its network of data centers and pipeline of power access that offer ready connectivity to the grid. The stock significantly outperformed, as Hut 8 in December signed its first leasing deal with a hyperscaler, with minimum projected net operating income over 15 years of nearly $7 billion, representing an unprecedented yield for any such agreement.

Detractors from relative performance:

  • AAON (AAON) is a manufacturer of heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) equipment for the industrial and commercial markets, most notably rooftop heating and cooling units, with a focus on custom design. In late 2021, AAON entered the high-growth cloud computing and AI data center HVAC markets with the acquisition of BASX Solutions (pronounced “Basics”). The stock pulled back during November’s market jitters over the sustainability of spending on the AI data center buildout, despite a large order backlog for its BASX segment. We view AAON as remaining well positioned to benefit from AI-driven spending, which has shown no signs of abating anytime soon. In addition, AAON’s commercial HVAC business has stabilized after a challenging period driven partly by a regulatory change in refrigerant guidelines and the company’s onboarding of a new software platform designed to enhance operational efficiency.
  • Ollie's Bargain Outlet (OLLI) is a chain of discount retail stores specializing in closeout and overstock name-brand products. The company has over 600 outlets across more than 30 states and is rapidly expanding its presence. The stock declined during the quarter despite Ollie’s posting earnings beat alongside strong sales growth driven by higher same-store results, while raising guidance.
  • Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM) is a chain of upscale grocery stores with a focus on healthy and organic foods. Sprouts reported revenues that were below expectations and lowered guidance, with management citing challenging comparisons and a weaker consumer. We view the market’s reaction as overstated. Sprouts continues to open new stores at a healthy pace and expand its ecommerce sales, supporting the company’s growth outlook.

Outlook

While we maintain a strong focus on individual stock selection, we, of course, monitor the macroeconomic backdrop, with an eye toward the potential impact on the companies in which we invest. In this vein, we see some potential tailwinds for company performance and investor risk sentiment in the coming year. Inflation has been more contained than originally feared in some quarters. In addition, some provisions in the tax and budget bill signed in July should provide a boost to consumer sentiment, and while there have been some signs of softening employment on the margins, layoffs have been measured and contained, while wage growth has remained reasonable. While advances from the ongoing investment in AI will take some time to spread throughout the economy, we expect the ultimate productivity enhancements to enable a higher rate of non-inflationary growth, which could allow the Fed to maintain rates at relatively low levels by historical standards.

We view the environment heading into 2026 as supportive of our focus on adding value by identifying the best growth opportunities in the small-cap space. The core tenet of our process is to focus on identifying innovators and durable business models, products and services. More broadly, we continue to look for companies in growing markets that take market share as they generate high or rising returns on investment. Such companies will grow earnings and cash flow faster than revenue. Our intrinsic value approach helps us determine which stocks to choose for total opportunity and risk/reward. We are finding numerous opportunities as innovation drives changes, and well-positioned companies can expand profits and ultimately free cash flow. Many of these small-cap growth stocks are attractively valued relative to their large-cap counterparts and have been overlooked by investors for some time.

data.title: Invesco Global Real Estate Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Manager perspective and outlook

  • Global macroeconomic conditions during the quarter were characterized by monetary easing, though inflationary pressures persisted across major economies. The US Federal Reserve cut the federal funds rate in October in response to labor market weakness, though inflation remained above target. In Europe, the European Central Bank approached the end of its rate cutting cycle, while the outlook for UK interest rates remained uncertain amid ongoing policy adjustments. Japan's economy grew above trend, with continued expectations for additional monetary tightening, in contrast to generally accommodative stances elsewhere. Against this backdrop, global listed real estate securities declined.
  • Global listed real estate equities ended the quarter trading even with their average underlying net asset value. However, this statistic hid wide dispersions in the valuations of countries and property types.
  • Our outlook for global listed real estate remains cautiously constructive as monetary policy has remained accommodative in major economies. Conditions should be most supportive for markets and sectors that have already experienced valuation declines and where tenant demand can provide a structural tailwind. This is especially true for landlords supplying high quality assets into supply-constrained or higher demand markets and thus commanding pricing power.

Portfolio positioning

The fund has remained broadly diversified across global listed real estate markets, with an overweight in Europe and an underweight in the Asia Pacific region. In the Americas, fund positioning signals our clear preference for structural growth sectors such as industrial, data centers and health care. The industrial REIT sector has been supported by gradually improving rent trends as new supply has declined and demand has normalized. Specialty REITs blend the stability of traditional operating platforms with the secular expansion of revenue streams from data centers, while dedicated data center REITs have continued to benefit from strong demand for cloud computing, artificial intelligence workloads and high density power capacity. The health care REIT sector provides defensive cash flows and has benefited from demographic tailwinds, in our view. By contrast, we have kept exposure to retail and residential REITs underweight given comparatively slower expected growth, and the office sector has remained underweight amid structural challenges.

In Europe, the fund is overweight in the UK, where it favors the self-storage and industrial REIT sectors as leasing momentum, operational visibility and attractive valuations offer in our view a supportive backdrop. Across Continental Europe, positioning emphasizes industrial and retail REITs with a focus on logistics operators that have been benefiting from resilient demand patterns in Central and Eastern Europe and Sweden. Retail REIT exposure includes leading operators in France and broader Europe, while the office sector is underweight given slower leasing decisions.

Within the Asia Pacific region, positioning remains focused on sectors with clear secular demand tailwinds, while balancing relative value opportunities across the region. In Australia, we maintain a constructive view on data center REITs, supported by strong digital infrastructure demand, and we retain exposure to industrial and residential REITs where fundamentals remain generally stable. In Hong Kong, overweights reflect in our view improving residential conditions, supportive policy developments and potential for strategic capital recycling to enhance asset quality. Japan allocations have benefited from healthy office fundamentals and reduced headwinds for industrial supply. We trimmed exposure to Singapore following strong performance but the fund has remained overweight in key retail and health care names within the country.

Performance highlights

The fund outperformed its benchmark for the quarter, with strong relative performance in North America being partially offset by weakness in Europe, Middle East and Africa and in the Asia Pacific region.

Contributors to performance

Within North America, the US was the primary contributor, led by the health care sector where stock selection had a notable positive effect, supported by positions in senior housing and triple-net health care operators, notably Omega Healthcare Investors. Not holding major life science REIT Alexandria Real Estate Equities benefited relative return as the REIT underperformed amid an ongoing supply/demand imbalance for lab space. The industrial sector added to relative return as it benefited from rising market rents and company-specific catalysts. An underweight in retail also benefited performance as the sector declined.

Positioning in Canada added to relative return due to a country underweight and favorable health care positioning. Not holding Canadian office REIT Allied Properties Real Estate Investment Trust (APYRF) added to relative return.

While overall relative results were driven by performance in North America, Japan made a positive contribution due to an overweight in major developers and favorable selection within JREITs. In France, an overweight in the retail sector, notably Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield (UNBLF), added to relative return.

Detractors from performance

Within Asia Pacific, Australia was the largest regional detractor, primarily due to an overweight in the data center sector, notably NEXTDC. Modest detractors among Singapore data centers and Hong Kong retail REITs also appeared to weigh on relative return.

In the Europe, Middle East and Africa region, having no exposure in Israel and an underweight in Switzerland detracted as both countries outperformed. Within the UK, the residential sector was the largest detractor due to a sharp decline in student housing REIT Unite Group (UTGPF) after disappointing leasing updates.

While overall US positioning was largely responsible for the fund's outperformance, some US sectors detracted. The specialty sector underperformed due to weakness in document storage and data center operator Iron Mountain, which was hampered by questions about long-term data center and AI trends despite its visible leasing strength in the near term. Stock selection in the residential sector also detracted due to weakness across large-cap apartments and single-family rentals, which struggled amid weaker labor market data.

Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio.
